retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin A1C -- Quality Control and Lab Safety
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory safety is equally critical to overall testing quality. All personnel must follow infection prevention protocols, including proper use of personal protective equipment and safe handling of biological specimens. Maintaining a safe and compliant laboratory environment helps ensure consistent performance while minimizing risks of errors or exposure. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin A1C -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The hemoglobin A1c test—also known as glycated hemoglobin, glycosylated hemoglobin, HbA1c, or simply A1c—is used to measure an individual's glucose control levels. The test shows average blood sugar levels over the past 90 days, expressed as a percentage. In addition, it can be used to diagnose diabetes mellitus. [1] Hemoglobin is a protein found exclusively in red blood cells, giving blood its bright red color. The primary role of hemoglobin is to carry oxygen from the lungs to all the cells in the body. Hemoglobin becomes glycated or coated with glucose from the bloodstream. As blood glucose levels increase, more glucose attaches to the hemoglobin protein, resulting in a higher A1c value. [2] Since red blood cells have an average lifespan of about 3 months, the A1c test measures hemoglobin levels in the bloodstream over this period, making it a reliable indicator of blood sugar control.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin A1C -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Several conditions can interfere with the accuracy of HbA1c testing. Individuals with sickle cell anemia, thalassemia, anemia, kidney failure, liver disease, or those receiving blood transfusions can experience altered results due to the longevity of the red blood cell. HbA1c measurement in these patients must be interpreted cautiously and confirmed with plasma glucose samples to diagnose diabetes mellitus. [22] A falsely low HbA1c value can result from several conditions, including high altitude, pregnancy, hemorrhage, blood transfusion, erythropoietin administration, iron supplementation, hemolytic anemia, chronic kidney failure, liver cirrhosis, alcoholism, sickle cell anemia, and spherocytosis. Vitamin C supplementation can increase or decrease HbA1c levels depending on the method used. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin A1C -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Immunoassay-based methods are also prevalent, using monoclonal antibodies that specifically bind to the Amadori product (ketoamine linkage) and distinct amino acid sequences at the N-terminal of the hemoglobin β-chain. A widely used format, latex agglutination inhibition, involves a synthetic polymer bearing HbA1c epitopes that competes with patient HbA1c for binding to antibody-coated latex beads. This competition reduces agglutination, which is quantifiable as decreased absorbance. Enzyme immunoassays using analogous antibodies exhibit acceptable precision and are typically calibrated to HPLC-derived values. These assays are highly specific, excluding labile intermediates; other glycated hemoglobin, such as HbA1a and HbA1b; and common hemoglobin variants, such as HbF, HbA2, HbS, and carbamylated hemoglobin. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Type 2 Diabetes -- Evaluation -- Glycated Hemoglobin (Hb) A1C
retrieved_docs.content: Hb A1C is costly and has many issues, as discussed below, including lower sensitivity. Hb A1C should be measured using the National Glycohemoglobin Standardization Program (NGSP) certified method standardized to Diabetes Control and Complications Trial (DCCT) assay. It is affected by numerous conditions such as sickle cell disease, pregnancy, hemodialysis, blood loss or transfusion, or erythropoietin therapy. It has not been well validated in non-white populations.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Utility of Fructosamine and Glycated Albumin -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: HbA 1c is a product of nonenzymatic glycation of hemoglobin. RBCs have a lifespan of approximately 90 to 120 days; hence, HbA 1c indicates the mean blood glucose concentration over the lifespan of the RBC. HbA 1c is influenced by conditions affecting RBC survival. Conditions causing low RBC turnover, such as untreated iron, vitamin B12, or folic acid deficiency anemias, result in falsely high HbA 1c values. On the other hand, conditions causing high RBC turnover, such as hemolytic anemia, can result in falsely low HbA 1c values. This phenomenon can occur in patients treated for iron, vitamin B12, or folate deficiencies, as well as in those treated with erythropoietin, such as individuals with chronic kidney disease.

retrieved_docs.content: There are a few different forms of normal hemoglobin in human blood. The percent prevalence of each hemoglobin type depends on the stage of development.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin is the chief erythrocyte protein that transports oxygen from the lungs to the tissues, carbon dioxide from the tissues to the lungs, and vasodilating nitrous oxide from nitrite to the blood vessels. Hemoglobin consists of α- and β-globins and an iron-bearing tetrapyrrole moiety. α- and β-globins influence oxygen loading and unloading and erythrocyte shape. Abnormal α- and β-globin result in hemoglobin dysfunction, misshapen erythrocytes, erythrocyte fragility, and the systemic symptoms seen in many hemoglobin disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Hemoglobin Synthesis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin is an oxygen-binding protein found in erythrocytes that transports oxygen from the lungs to tissues. Each hemoglobin molecule is a tetramer made of four polypeptide globin chains. Each globin subunit contains a heme moiety formed of an organic protoporphyrin ring and a central iron ion in the ferrous state (Fe2+). The iron molecule in each heme moiety can bind and unbind oxygen, allowing for oxygen transport in the body. The most common type of hemoglobin in the adult is HbA, which comprises two alpha-globin and two beta-globin subunits. Different globin genes encode each type of globin subunit. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A study showed that in a diabetic patient presenting with "HbS" or "HbC," there is a considerable false decrease in the values for glycosylated hemoglobin (HbA1c) despite persistent hyperglycemia and this decrease is proportional to the percentage of abnormal hemoglobin. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin C Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The mutation present in some of the hemoglobinopathies (Hb S, Hb C, Hb E) may be an evolutionary modification due to the effect of some selective external forces like malaria as in the heterozygous form, these mutations protect carriers from dying of malarial infection. The cohort study was done on three hundred children in Bandiagara, Mali, found out that both HbC and HbS traits provide protection from clinical falciparum malaria. [1] One in-vitro study has proposed the mechanism that the hemoglobinopathies (HbS, HbC) impair protein export in Plasmodium falciparum infected erythrocytes. This provides a mechanistic explanation for reduced disease mediating cytoadherence of parasitized hemoglobinopathic erythrocytes. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemoglobin A1C -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Conversely, a falsely high HbA1c value can be due to insufficient iron in the blood. This condition can result from iron deficiency anemia, infection-induced or tumor-induced anemia. In addition, hemoglobinopathies such as thalassemia and B12 deficiency can cause a falsely high HbA1c value. [25] [26] Other causes of falsely high HbA1c levels include hypertriglyceridemia, organ transplantation, and hyperglycation in certain ethnic groups. Medications such as immunosuppressants and protease inhibitors can sometimes lead to a falsely high HbA1c value. [22] [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Utility of Fructosamine and Glycated Albumin -- Clinical Significance -- Monitoring Glucose Control in Diabetes Mellitus
retrieved_docs.content: Fructosamine has largely been used as an alternative to HbA 1c in certain conditions that limit the reliability of HbA 1c , such as hemoglobin variants and alterations in erythrocyte lifespan. Unlike HbA 1c , fructosamine and glycated albumin are unaffected by hemoglobin levels or RBC characteristics, making them reliable markers in conditions such as hemoglobinopathies, sickle cell anemia, and anemias due to iron, vitamin B12, or folate deficiency. Additionally, fructosamine has clinical utility in conditions where information regarding short-term glucose control is important in the management of the patient, such as in pregnancy or recent medication adjustment. Fructosamine and glycated albumin can also be useful in monitoring individuals with diabetes mellitus with fluctuating or poorly controlled diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1524
retrieved_docs.content: Darbepoetin alfa is classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as a member of the “other antianemic preparations” group, indicating its role as an erythropoiesis‑stimulating agent used to treat various forms of anemia. This classification situates darbepoetin alfa among drugs that are specifically designed to increase red blood cell production, distinguishing it from iron supplements or other traditional antianemic therapies. Recognizing darbepoetin alfa’s placement in the ATC category of other antianemic preparations helps clinicians understand its therapeutic purpose, mechanism of action, and appropriate use in managing anemia‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2746
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropoietin is classified as a member of the “other antianemic preparations” group within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system, indicating its role as an antianemic agent used to treat various forms of anemia. This categorization places erythropoietin among drugs that are not included in the primary iron‑based or vitamin‑based antianemic classes, highlighting its distinct mechanism of stimulating red blood cell production. Understanding erythropoietin’s placement in the ATC system helps clinicians recognize its therapeutic purpose and differentiate it from other anemia treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions comprise a broad therapeutic class that includes both irrigating solutions and intravenous (IV) solutions used for parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, nutrients, and plasma‑derived or synthetic colloids. Within this hierarchy, the subcategory “irrigating solutions, blood substitutes and perfusion” contains other irrigating solutions (e.g., glycin) and salt irrigating solutions (e.g., sodium bicarbonate). The IV‑solution branch contains formulations that affect electrolyte balance; the carbohydrate/electrolyte group links agents that combine electrolytes with carbohydrates, antidiarrheals, and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective drugs. The “blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions” subgroup includes O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin, dextran, and hydroxyethyl‑starch. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1568
retrieved_docs.content: O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin and hydroxyethylový škrob are both classified as members of the category “blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions.” As blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions, these agents are designed to mimic or augment the physiological functions of blood components or plasma proteins, providing therapeutic options when conventional blood transfusion is unavailable or when specific protein supplementation is needed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1396
retrieved_docs.content: O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin and dextran are both classified as members of the group “blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions,” indicating that they serve as artificial blood products or plasma protein derivatives used to replace or augment the functions of red blood cells or plasma proteins in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1726
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropoetin is a biologically active peptide that falls under the broader category of “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein.” Within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system, erythropoetin is a member of the group labeled “other antianemic preparations in ATC,” reflecting its use as a protein‑based therapy to stimulate red blood cell production in the treatment of anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1912
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate are both members of the therapeutic class identified as iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations. This class comprises oral formulations that deliver trivalent iron to patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, thereby replenishing depleted iron stores and supporting hemoglobin synthesis. By belonging to the same category, ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate share a common mechanism of action and are used interchangeably as oral options for correcting iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1349
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol is classified as a hematinic, indicating that it belongs to the group of agents used to increase hemoglobin levels and treat iron‑deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 345
retrieved_docs.content: O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin is classified as a member of the broader category of blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions, indicating that it functions as a hemoglobin‑based oxygen carrier used to replace or augment blood components.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 297
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and plasma protein fractions comprise O‑raffinose cross‑linked human hemoglobin and hydroxyethylový škrob as members of this product class. Hydroxyethylový škrob, beta‑D‑glucopyranose, metylovaný pektin and migalastat are each classified within the carbohydrate (sacharidy) category, indicating that they share fundamental sugar‑based chemical characteristics. Metylovaný pektin functions as an inactive ingredient in the formulation of cholestyramine resin 4000 MG Powder for Oral Suspension, linking a carbohydrate excipient to a bile‑acid‑binding therapeutic agent. Both aztreonam 1000 MG Injection and cholestyramine resin 4000 MG Powder for Oral Suspension specify gram as the numerator unit for their presentation strength, reflecting a common dosing metric across these distinct pharmaceutical products. Together, these relationships illustrate how various hemoglobin‑based blood substitutes, carbohydrate‑derived excipients, and antimicrobial or lipid‑lowering agents are organized by class, composition, and dosage conventions, which is essential for formulation design, regulatory classification, and patient‑centered medication management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 62
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS represent a broad therapeutic class that encompasses agents used to treat or prevent anemia, including folic acid and vitamin B12 preparations. Within this larger group, the subclass BLOOD AND BLOOD FORMING ORGAN DRUGS is classified under the ATC category “other hematological agents,” and these drugs are characterized by hemostatic effects. The hemostatic actions of this subclass are further related to specific hemostatics such as vitamin K and related compounds. The ATC group “other hematological agents” also includes several distinct therapeutic and biochemical categories. ENZYMES, HEMATOLOGICAL are defined as a subclass of other hematological agents and contain deoxyribonucleases as member enzymes. Deoxyribonucleases, in turn, have a narrower member, streptodornase, which is a specific enzymatic preparation. HEME PRODUCTS (pharmacologic substances) are another subclass within the same ATC group, reflecting the use of heme‑derived compounds in hematologic
